---
title: "chatlas"
subtitle: "Programming with LLMs in Python"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, theme.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    incremental: false
    transition: fade
    code-line-numbers: false
---

## {.center}

![](images/chatlas.png){height="250px" fig-align="center"}

One consistent interface to **any** LLM provider.

::: notes
chatlas is the foundation everything else builds on. It gives you one Python
API that works the same whether you're hitting Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, or
a local model. You learn one set of patterns, and you can swap providers with
a single line change.
:::

## Hello, chatlas {.center}

```{.python}
import chatlas as ctl

chat = ctl.ChatAnthropic()
chat.chat("What is the most popular Python package for data analysis?")
```

::: {.fragment}
That's it. One import, two lines, streaming output.
:::

::: notes
Live demo: run this in a notebook or console. The output streams in real-time.
Point out that `chat.chat()` both sends the message AND echoes the response —
it's designed for interactive exploration.

Mention: if they're using OpenAI, just swap to `ctl.ChatOpenAI()`. Local
model? `ctl.ChatOllama()` or `ctl.ChatLMStudio()`. The rest of the code
stays identical.
:::

## Bring your own provider {.center}

```{.python}
# Hosted
chat = ctl.ChatAnthropic()          # Claude
chat = ctl.ChatOpenAI()             # GPT
chat = ctl.ChatGoogle()             # Gemini

# Local (no API key needed)
chat = ctl.ChatOllama()             # Ollama
chat = ctl.ChatLMStudio()           # LM Studio

# Pick via a single string
chat = ctl.ChatAuto("anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6")
```

Same API, every provider. Use whatever you have today.

::: notes
Don't linger here — just establish that provider choice is a one-line decision.
`ChatAuto` is useful when you want to parameterize the provider (e.g., from
an env var or config file). For the rest of the slides we'll use
`ChatAnthropic()` but they should substitute their own.
:::

## System prompt {.center}

```{.python}
chat = ctl.ChatAnthropic(
    system_prompt=(
        "You are a data science tutor. "
        "Explain concepts simply, using analogies from everyday life."
    ),
)

chat.chat("What is a p-value?")
```

::: {.fragment}
The system prompt is your **invisible hand** — it shapes every response
without the user seeing it.
:::

::: notes
Live demo: run this, then run the same question without a system prompt to
show the contrast. The system prompt is sent with every request (LLMs are
stateless between calls). This is the simplest form of "context engineering"
— the theme of the whole day.

Key point: the system prompt is where *you the developer* talk to the model.
The user prompt is where *the end user* talks to the model. Keeping these
separate is a fundamental design principle.
:::

## Multi-turn: the chat remembers {.center}

```{.python}
chat.chat("What about confidence intervals?")
```

```{.python}
chat.chat("How do those two concepts relate?")
```

::: {.fragment}
`Chat` objects are **stateful** — they accumulate conversation history.

```{.python}
chat.get_turns()          # inspect the history
chat.set_turns([])        # reset to start fresh
```
:::

::: notes
This is a key thing for newcomers to understand: the Chat object is managing
the conversation state for you. Each call to .chat() appends the user message
and the assistant response to an internal list of turns. When the next request
goes out, the full history goes with it.

This is why the model can answer "how do those two concepts relate?" — it has
the full conversation in context. If you want a fresh start, `set_turns([])`
clears the history.
:::

## `.chat()` vs `.stream()` {.center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

### `.chat()` — interactive

```{.python}
# Blocks, auto-echoes output
chat.chat("Explain PCA")
```

Great for **notebooks** and **exploration**.

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

### `.stream()` — for apps

```{.python}
# Returns a generator you control
for chunk in chat.stream("Explain PCA"):
    print(chunk, end="")
```

Great for **Shiny**, **FastAPI**, etc.

:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
Both have async variants: `.chat_async()` and `.stream_async()`
:::

::: notes
This distinction matters when we get to shinychat. In an app, you don't want
to block the event loop — you want to stream chunks to the UI as they arrive.
`.stream()` gives you that control. `.chat()` is the convenience wrapper for
when you're just exploring.

For multi-user web apps, always use the async variants — they won't block
other users' requests.
:::

## Tool calling: the big idea {.center}

LLMs can't **do** things — but they can **ask you** to do things.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 8
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as You
    participant L as LLM
    participant T as Your Tool

    U->>L: "What's the weather in Paris?"
    L->>T: get_weather(city="Paris")
    T-->>L: {"temp": 18, "condition": "cloudy"}
    L->>U: "It's 18°C and cloudy in Paris."
```

The model **decides** what to call. Your code **executes** it.

::: notes
This is the conceptual slide — make sure they understand the flow before
seeing code. The LLM never actually calls the function itself. It outputs a
structured "please call this function with these arguments" message. chatlas
intercepts that, calls your Python function, sends the result back to the
model, and the model incorporates it into its response.

This is the "harness" concept from the foundations section in action.
:::

## Your first tool {.center}

```{.python}
def get_current_weather(lat: float, lng: float):
    """Get the current weather for a location.

    Parameters
    ----------
    lat
        The latitude.
    lng
        The longitude.
    """
    import requests
    resp = requests.get(
        "https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast",
        params={"latitude": lat, "longitude": lng, "current": "temperature_2m"},
    )
    return resp.json()["current"]

chat = ctl.ChatAnthropic()
chat.register_tool(get_current_weather)
chat.chat("What's the weather in Paris?")
```

::: notes
Live demo. Walk through the key ingredients:

1. **Type hints** (lat: float, lng: float) — required. The model uses these to
   know what arguments to pass.
2. **Docstring** — required. This is how the model understands what the tool
   does and when to use it.
3. **`register_tool()`** — hands the tool to the chat. chatlas automatically
   converts the function signature + docstring into the tool schema the model
   expects.
4. **The loop is automatic** — chatlas handles the tool call, sends the result
   back, and lets the model generate a final response. You don't write any
   loop code.

Point out: the model figured out Paris's lat/lng on its own. That's the
"language → code" strength we talked about in foundations.
:::

## What just happened? {.center}

When you ran `chat.chat(...)`, chatlas ran this loop for you:

::: {.incremental}
1. Sent your message **+** the tool schema to the model
2. Model responded: _"call `get_current_weather(48.85, 2.35)`"_
3. chatlas **called your function** with those arguments
4. Sent the result back to the model
5. Model responded with a **natural language** answer
:::

::: {.fragment}
Steps 2–4 can repeat — the model can call **multiple tools** in sequence.
:::

::: notes
This is the "under the hood" explanation. Emphasize that they never had to
write any of this orchestration code — chatlas does it. But understanding the
loop is important because it explains why tools need good docstrings (the
model reads them) and why errors in tools don't crash the program (chatlas
catches them and sends the error back to the model).
:::

## Tools for data scientists {.center}

```{.python}
import pandas as pd

tips = pd.read_csv(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mwaskom/seaborn-data/master/tips.csv"
)

def query_tips(code: str):
    """Execute Python code to answer a question about the tips dataset.

    Parameters
    ----------
    code
        Python code to run. The `tips` DataFrame and `pd` (pandas) are available.
    """
    return str(eval(code, {"tips": tips, "pd": pd}))

chat = ctl.ChatAnthropic()
chat.register_tool(query_tips)
chat.chat("What's the average tip percentage by day of the week?")
```

::: notes
This is the "aha" moment for data scientists in the room. The model writes
pandas code, chatlas executes it, and the model interprets the result in
natural language. This is a preview of what querychat does at scale — but
here it's just 10 lines of code.

Live demo: run this, then ask a follow-up like "which day has the highest
total bill?" to show multi-turn tool use.

SAFETY NOTE: `eval()` here is fine for a local demo, but you'd never do this
in production. querychat uses a sandboxed approach — we'll cover that later.
:::

## Built-in tools {.center}

```{.python}
from chatlas import tool_web_search

chat = ctl.ChatAnthropic()
chat.register_tool(tool_web_search())

chat.chat("What were the top Python packages released this month?")
```

::: {.fragment}
No API key, no configuration — the provider handles it natively.
:::

::: notes
chatlas ships built-in tools that use the provider's native capabilities.
`tool_web_search()` is the most useful — it lets the model search the web
for current information. Supported by Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google.

There's also `tool_web_fetch()` for reading specific URLs (Anthropic and
Google only).

The nice thing: these aren't custom implementations — they use the provider's
own grounded search, which means citations and source attribution come free.
:::

## Quick tour: more capabilities {.center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Structured output
```{.python}
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Person(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int

chat.chat_structured(
    "Susan is 13 years old",
    data_model=Person,
)
# → Person(name='Susan', age=13)
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

### MCP tools
```{.python}
await chat.register_mcp_tools_stdio_async(
    command="uvx",
    args=["mcp-server-fetch"],
)
```

### Images & PDFs
```{.python}
chat.chat(
    ctl.content_image_file("chart.png"),
    "Describe this chart.",
)
```

:::
:::

::: notes
Don't go deep on any of these — just plant the seed. Structured output is
incredibly useful for extraction pipelines. MCP lets you connect to any
MCP-compatible tool server. Multi-modal input (images, PDFs) opens up
document analysis workflows.

The point: chatlas is a full-featured LLM client, not a toy. But today we're
focusing on the core loop (chat + tools) because that's what shinychat and
querychat build on.
:::

## 🛠️ Hands-on: build with chatlas {.center}

**~15 minutes** — pick your adventure:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

### 🧪 Option A: Extend an example

Start from the exercise file and:

1. Add a **system prompt** that sets a persona
2. Add a **second tool** (e.g., a unit converter, a dice roller, etc.)
3. **Bonus**: add `tool_web_search()`

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

### 🤖 Option B: Vibe-code it

Open your favorite chatbot and prompt:

_"Create a chatlas Python app that [your idea here]"_

See how far you get — then **read** and **fix** what it generates.

:::
:::

Exercise file: **`exercises/01-chatlas-starter.py`**

::: notes
Option A is the guided path — they start from working code and extend it.
Option B is the self-directed path — they use an LLM to generate chatlas code
and see whether it works. Option B is a meta-learning exercise: they're
learning to evaluate LLM-generated code, which is exactly the "human in the
loop" principle from the foundations section.

Float around the room and help. Common stumbles:
- Forgetting type hints or docstrings on tools
- Using `chat.stream()` when they meant `chat.chat()` in a script
- API key not set as env var

Wrap up by asking 1-2 people to share what they built.
:::

# ☕ Break — 5 min {.center background-color="#0e3b5c"}

::: notes
Next up: shinychat — turning chatlas into a real chatbot UI. Make sure everyone
has a working chatlas example before moving on.
:::
